explicit_text: I come from Okayama Prefecture, a town with a population of around 5,000 to 6,000. I lived on the edge of the town, and my family were farmers. There were eight children in my family, and I was the second child. Growing up there, I attended local schools before moving on to normal school for teacher training.
implicit_text: I come from a rural area in western Japan, a town with a population of around 5,000 to 6,000. I lived on the edge of the town, and my family were farmers. There were eight children in my family, and I was the second child. Growing up there, I attended local schools before moving on to normal school for teacher training.

explicit_text: I remember the Sino-Japanese War vaguely from my childhood. It was a significant event during my early years in Japan, but I don't recall many details. It was one of the many conflicts that shaped the era I grew up in before coming to America.
implicit_text: I remember a major conflict between Japan and China vaguely from my childhood. It was a significant event during my early years in Japan, but I don't recall many details. It was one of the many conflicts that shaped the era I grew up in before coming to America.

explicit_text: The Russo-Japanese War happened when I was about five years old. I don't remember much, but I do recall a lantern parade that must have been to celebrate the end of the war. We also sang a song that went 'Japanese Won, Japanese Won, Russo Lost,' which was quite memorable.
implicit_text: The war between Russia and Japan happened when I was about five years old. I don't remember much, but I do recall a lantern parade that must have been to celebrate the end of the war. We also sang a song that went 'Japanese Won, Japanese Won, Russo Lost,' which was quite memorable.

explicit_text: During World War I, I was attending high school. I remember very little about the war itself, but I do recall the fall of Tsingtao vividly. One day in the fall, after a picnic in the mountains, we returned by boat at night to find the town excited with lanterns lit and people celebrating the capture of Tsingtao from Germany.
implicit_text: During the global conflict of 1914-1918, I was attending high school. I remember very little about the war itself, but I do recall the fall of a German-held port city vividly. One day in the fall, after a picnic in the mountains, we returned by boat at night to find the town excited with lanterns lit and people celebrating the capture of that city from Germany.

explicit_text: Tsingtao was a German-occupied territory that Japan attacked and took during World War I. I remember the day it fell clearly; it was in the fall when I was in high school. The town was very excited, with lanterns lit and celebrations all around as people welcomed the news.
implicit_text: The German-occupied port city in China was attacked and taken by Japan during the First World War. I remember the day it fell clearly; it was in the fall when I was in high school. The town was very excited, with lanterns lit and celebrations all around as people welcomed the news.

explicit_text: Port Arthur was a place that Russia had taken before Japan attacked it. During the time around World War I, Japan was involved in conflicts over territories like Port Arthur and Tsingtao. Although I was not directly involved, these events were part of the larger historical context of my youth.
implicit_text: There was a strategic port city that Russia had taken before Japan attacked it. During the time around the First World War, Japan was involved in conflicts over territories like that port and the German-held city in China. Although I was not directly involved, these events were part of the larger historical context of my youth.

explicit_text: I was already in the United States during the Manchu Conflict, so I don't have personal memories of it. However, my husband's brother suggested doing business either in Manchuria or America, but my husband preferred to return to America. Manchuria was part of the broader geopolitical issues of that time.
implicit_text: I was already in the United States during the conflict in northeastern China, so I don't have personal memories of it. explicit_text: After graduating from high school, I attended a normal school called Shihan Nibu to become a teacher. Unlike many who entered after eighth grade, I entered after completing four years of high school and had to learn teaching skills in just one year. It was intense, covering child psychology, education history, and classroom management.

explicit_text: Koto Shihan was a teachers college different from the normal school I attended. Graduates from Koto Shihan could teach in high schools, which was a higher qualification. My high school teacher once asked if I would try to go there, but my family situation and the fact that many girls married early made me decide that normal school was enough.

explicit_text: I was living in Japan during the Great Kantō earthquake. There were many rumors about Koreans rioting, which caused confusion and fear. My brother, who lived in Tokyo, came home during the chaos and talked about opportunities to make money, but our family decided to do nothing and avoid trouble.
implicit_text: I was living in Japan during the massive earthquake that struck the Kantō region. There were many rumors about Koreans rioting, which caused confusion and fear. My brother, who lived in Tokyo, came home during the chaos and talked about opportunities to make money, but our family decided to do nothing and avoid trouble.

explicit_text: Obon was one of the happy times I remember in Japan; it is a Buddhist fall ceremony. Along with New Year's and shrine festivals, Obon was a time when families gathered and celebrated. These events brought joy and a sense of community during my childhood.
implicit_text: One of the happy times I remember in Japan was the Buddhist fall ceremony. Along with New Year's and shrine festivals, this was a time when families gathered and celebrated. These events brought joy and a sense of community during my childhood.

explicit_text: During New Year's in Japan, we played Hanetsuki, which is similar to badminton. It was one of the traditional games that made the holiday special and fun. I enjoyed these games along with others like Sugoroku and Karuta during the festive season.
implicit_text: During New Year's in Japan, we played a traditional game similar to badminton. It was one of the games that made the holiday special and fun. I enjoyed these games along with others like Sugoroku and Karuta during the festive season.

explicit_text: Sugoroku was a game we played during New Year's celebrations, similar to Monopoly. It was part of the joyful activities that marked the holiday season in Japan. Along with Hanetsuki and Karuta, it helped make those times memorable for me.
implicit_text: We played a board game similar to Monopoly during New Year's celebrations. It was part of the joyful activities that marked the holiday season in Japan. Along with Hanetsuki and Karuta, it helped make those times memorable for me.

explicit_text: Karuta was a card game we played during New Year's, involving one hundred poems by one hundred poets. I wasn't very good at it, but it was a cherished part of the holiday festivities. It connected me to Japanese culture and poetry even as a child.

explicit_text: Otoshidama was a custom where children went around saying 'Happy New Year' and received small amounts of money from neighbors and relatives. I remember the word but not clearly what I did during it. It was a part of the New Year's celebrations in my hometown.

explicit_text: Hachiman was one of the festivals we had, dedicated to a warrior god, possibly worshipping Genji, an old noble warrior. It was not as large as some other festivals but still part of our cultural and religious life. These festivals were important community events in my youth.
implicit_text: One of the festivals we had was dedicated to a warrior god, possibly worshipping an old noble warrior from Japanese history. It was not as large as some other festivals but still part of our cultural and religious life. These festivals were important community events in my youth.

explicit_text: Oinari was the largest festival in my area, dedicated to the fox spirit. I remember hearing that people put fried bean curds in front of the shrine for the fox to eat. The shrine priest was also a high school teacher who taught Japanese, which made the festival feel connected to our daily lives.

explicit_text: Uji-Gami was one of the native gods' festivals we had, though it was smaller compared to Oinari or Hachiman. It was part of the local religious celebrations that marked the seasons and brought people together. These festivals were a meaningful part of my upbringing in Japan.

explicit_text: My family believed in Shingon Shu, one of the oldest Buddhist sects in Japan. It was established in the Koya Mountains, and though I have forgotten many details, it was an important part of our spiritual life. This sect influenced the festivals and ceremonies we observed growing up.

explicit_text: The Koya Mountains were the place where the Shingon Buddhist sect was established. While I don't remember many details, I know it was significant as the origin of the sect my family followed. It represents a spiritual heritage that shaped our religious practices.

explicit_text: I heard about Christianity as a child from a playmate whose father was a Christian minister. She often talked about her grandmother being a star in the sky. I also knew a farmer called Yaso Masa, which meant Christian in Japanese, but I never attended church myself, though I read a gospel once, probably given by my brother.

explicit_text: The Come Sodo, or rice riot, happened just after World War I ended. It was a time when hungry poor people looted the rich, but it did not happen in my town. It was a social trouble that marked the difficult times in Japan before I came to America.
implicit_text: There was a rice riot just after the First World War ended. It was a time when hungry poor people looted the rich, but it did not happen in my town. It was a social trouble that marked the difficult times in Japan before I came to America.

explicit_text: I came to the United States after getting married because there were few job opportunities in Japan. My husband had worked in the express business and later in a salmon cannery here. We first landed in Seattle, then went to Portland and Astoria, Oregon, where I experienced life in the cannery and met people from different backgrounds.
implicit_text: I came to North America after getting married because there were few job opportunities in Japan. My husband had worked in the express business and later in a salmon cannery here. We first landed in a major West Coast city, then went to other places in Oregon, where I experienced life in the cannery and met people from different backgrounds.

explicit_text: Our ship first stopped at Seattle when we arrived in the United States. I remember going through immigration there and being impressed by the huge market with cantaloupes stacked like mountains. It was my first impression of America, though I was somewhat hazy about my feelings at the time.
implicit_text: Our ship first stopped at a large port city in the Pacific Northwest when we arrived in North America. explicit_text: After Seattle, we went to Portland, Oregon, where we visited my husband's old acquaintances. It was part of our initial journey in America before settling in Astoria. Portland was a place where we connected with familiar people and adjusted to life here.
implicit_text: After the first city, we went to a city in Oregon where we visited my husband's old acquaintances. It was part of our initial journey in this country before settling in a smaller town. That city was a place where we connected with familiar people and adjusted to life here.

explicit_text: In Astoria, Oregon, my husband worked at a salmon cannery, and I stayed in the married couples' housing. I tried working there for one day but mostly spent time reading and knitting. The cannery was run by Japanese, and the food was cooked by a Chinese cook, which I found difficult to eat at first.

explicit_text: On the ship to America, I met a man from Kyushu who was educated and fine, and he was someone my husband knew. Although I don't remember much about him now, he was a comforting presence during the voyage. Kyushu was far from my hometown, but it connected me to others on the journey.
